verses.text: Now Jericho was straitly shut up because of the children of Israel: none went out, none came in. Guidance: A double-locked city, gates barred, citizens trapped inside. The verse reads like a chess match: Israel cannot starve them out fast, Jericho cannot sortie out. But God has a plan that does not depend on either move. Sometimes the apparent stalemate is the prelude to the most spectacular act of grace.

The LORD said unto Joshua, See, I have given into thine hand Jericho, the king thereof, and the mighty men of valour. Ye shall compass the city, all ye men of war, and go round about the city once. Thus shalt thou do six days. Seven priests shall bear before the ark seven trumpets of rams' horns: on the seventh day ye shall compass the city seven times, and the priests shall blow with the trumpets. When they make a long blast with the ram's horn, all the people shall shout with a great shout; and the wall of the city shall fall down flat, and the people shall ascend up every man straight before him. Guidance: 'I have given' — past tense, before the first foot is lifted. The strategy is liturgy, not siege. Seven priests, seven trumpets, seven days, seven laps. A military operation that looks like a worship procession. The army's task is to march in silence; God's task is to bring the walls down. The architecture of the conquest is decided before the architecture of Jericho fails.

Joshua called the priests: Take up the ark of the covenant, and let seven priests bear seven trumpets of rams' horns before the ark of the LORD. He said unto the people: Pass on, compass the city, let him that is armed pass on before the ark of the LORD. Guidance: Joshua does not adjust the plan. He repeats it. The trumpets are not silver war horns — they are ram's horns, jubilee horns, the instruments that announced liberty. The instrument that levels Jericho is the same instrument that frees slaves: the LORD's war is a war of liberation, even of the land itself.

Joshua called the priests: Take up the ark of the covenant, and let seven priests bear seven trumpets of rams' horns before the ark of the LORD. He said unto the people: Pass on, compass the city, let him that is armed pass on before the ark of the LORD. Guidance: Joshua does not adjust the plan. He repeats it. The trumpets are not silver war horns — they are ram's horns, jubilee horns, the instruments that announced liberty. The instrument that levels Jericho is the same instrument that frees slaves: the LORD's war is a war of liberation, even of the land itself.

When Joshua had spoken unto the people, the seven priests bearing the seven trumpets of rams' horns passed on before the LORD, and blew with the trumpets: the ark of the covenant of the LORD followed them. The armed men went before the priests that blew with the trumpets, the rereward came after the ark, the priests going on and blowing. Joshua had commanded the people: Ye shall not shout, nor make any noise, neither shall any word proceed out of your mouth, until the day I bid you shout; then shall ye shout. So the ark of the LORD compassed the city, going about it once: they came into the camp, and lodged. Guidance: A silent army marching behind a deliberately noisy ark. The whole nation holds its tongue while the rams' horns hold the air. Restraint of speech is the spiritual discipline of week one. Sometimes God asks not for words from us, only for steps. Then He saves the shout for His own appointed hour.

Joshua rose early in the morning, the priests took up the ark of the LORD. The seven priests bearing seven trumpets of rams' horns before the ark of the LORD went on continually, blew with the trumpets: armed men went before them; the rereward came after the ark, the priests going on and blowing. The second day they compassed the city once, returned into the camp: so they did six days. Guidance: Six days the same lap. Same dust, same gates, same hot sun, same suspicious faces watching from the walls. God could have brought Jericho down on day one, but He chose day seven. There is a discipline of repetition that nothing else can teach — the slow conviction that obedience is enough on its own, even when the walls don't move.

On the seventh day, they rose early about the dawning of the day, and compassed the city after the same manner seven times: only on that day they compassed the city seven times. At the seventh time, when the priests blew with the trumpets, Joshua said unto the people: Shout; for the LORD hath given you the city. Guidance: Day seven was the longest day. Seven laps of a 9-acre city — maybe four hours of walking with no result yet. Then the cue: 'Shout; for the LORD hath given.' Not 'will give,' but 'hath.' Israel shouts at a wall that is still standing — faith always speaks in the past tense about what God has promised.

The city shall be accursed, even it, and all therein, to the LORD: only Rahab the harlot shall live, she and all that are with her in the house, because she hid the messengers we sent. Keep yourselves from the accursed thing, lest ye make yourselves accursed, when ye take of it, and make the camp of Israel a curse, and trouble it. All the silver, gold, and vessels of brass and iron, are consecrated unto the LORD: they shall come into the treasury of the LORD. Guidance: The ban — herem — gives the whole city to God. Nothing for personal souvenirs. Two exceptions are named: Rahab's house is spared (faith trumps tribe), and the metals go to the treasury (consecrated, not destroyed). 'Lest ye make yourselves accursed' — a warning that lands directly in Achan's chapter, chapter 7. The forbidden cloak is already being woven in someone's mind.

So the people shouted when the priests blew with the trumpets: when the people heard the sound of the trumpet, and the people shouted with a great shout, the wall fell down flat, so that the people went up into the city, every man straight before him, and they took the city. They utterly destroyed all that was in the city, both man and woman, young and old, and ox, and sheep, and ass, with the edge of the sword. Guidance: The wall does not crack — it falls flat. The breach is so complete that every soldier walks 'straight before him,' no detours. And then the harshness: total destruction. This is judgment, not raiding. The text doesn't soften it; it dates Canaan's iniquity at Gen 15:16 — God had waited four centuries before judgment fell. Mercy delayed for generations is not mercy denied; it is mercy stretched as far as righteousness allows.

Joshua had said unto the two spies: Go into the harlot's house, bring out thence the woman, and all that she hath, as ye sware unto her. The young men brought out Rahab, her father, mother, brethren, all she had; brought out all her kindred, and left them without the camp of Israel. They burnt the city with fire, and all therein: only the silver, gold, and vessels of brass and iron, they put into the treasury of the house of the LORD. Joshua saved Rahab the harlot alive, and her father's household, and all she had; she dwelleth in Israel even unto this day; because she hid the messengers Joshua sent to spy out Jericho. Guidance: The two spies do not forget. While the city is sacked, they make a beeline for the only safe house in town. Rahab's scarlet thread held: every name on her list walks out. 'She dwelleth in Israel unto this day' — Matthew 1:5 will say her great-grandson is David, and her great-great-great-…-grandson is Christ. The harlot becomes a matriarch of the Messiah.

Joshua adjured them at that time, saying: Cursed be the man before the LORD that riseth up and buildeth this city Jericho: he shall lay the foundation thereof in his firstborn, and in his youngest son shall he set up the gates of it. So the LORD was with Joshua; his fame was noised throughout all the country. Guidance: Joshua's curse hangs over the ruins for centuries — fulfilled in 1 Kings 16:34, when Hiel the Bethelite rebuilds Jericho and loses two sons exactly as named. God's words do not expire. The chapter closes the way chapter 3 began: 'the LORD was with Joshua.' The same promise, now ratified by a wall of rubble.
